items.mistake: Treating eigenvectors from `np.linalg.eig` as unique without checking scaling or sign
items.fix: Normalize eigenvectors for comparison; remember v and -v (and any non-zero scalar multiple) are the same eigenvector.
items.why: Libraries return arbitrary normalized columns; comparing raw columns across runs can look inconsistent even when mathematically equivalent.
items.example: If v = [1, 1] is an eigenvector, so is [-2, -2]; cosine similarity between them is 1.0 after normalization.

items.mistake: Using `eig` instead of `eigh` for symmetric covariance matrices
items.fix: Call `np.linalg.eigh` when A is symmetric; it guarantees real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors up to numerical tolerance.
items.why: `eig` may return small imaginary parts from floating error and non-orthogonal vectors for nearly symmetric matrices.
items.example: For C = X.T @ X / (n-1), `eigh(C)` returns sorted real eigenvalues; `eig(C)` may show 1e-16j imaginary noise.

items.mistake: Assuming repeated eigenvalues imply duplicate eigenvector directions
items.fix: Check dimension of the eigenspace (geometric multiplicity), not just algebraic multiplicity of the root.
items.why: A repeated root can still have multiple independent eigenvectors (identity matrix) or only one (defective Jordan block).
items.example: I₂ has eigenvalue 1 with algebraic multiplicity 2 and a 2D eigenspace; a shear matrix can have eigenvalue 1 with only a 1D eigenspace.
